metadata.title: Why a cheap-worker crew reads 9 papers for a third less — at identical quality
metadata.purpose: Decompose by token-intensity — high-throughput extraction needs volume not reasoning (cheap models suffice); frontier intelligence concentrates only at planning and synthesis, where it sets outcome quality.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro af_kore) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.exclusions: specific model prices, routing algorithms, model-selection criteria
metadata.visual_object: Cost bars (frontier-only vs hybrid) with fan-out arrows from coordinator to 3 workers and back to a synthesis node
metadata.hook: Running a frontier model on every subtask wastes money; running cheap models everywhere loses quality — the split is not obvious.

beats.narration_text: Summarize 9 papers. Frontier-only: $0.90. Hybrid: frontier coordinator plans ($0.05), 3 cheap workers each read 3 papers ($0.15 each), frontier synthesizes ($0.10) = $0.60. A quality grader scores both 8.7/10.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: A frontier coordinator with cheap parallel workers should predict matched quality at lower cost; running frontier on every subtask did not; why?
Running a frontier model on every subtask wastes money; running cheap models everywhere loses quality — the split is not obvious.

Decompose by token-intensity — high-throughput extraction needs volume not reasoning (cheap models suffice); frontier intelligence concentrates only at planning and synthesis, where it sets outcome quality.

beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: 9 papers → $0.90 solo vs $0.60 hybrid; grader 8.7/10 both; 33% saved at zero quality cost.

Decompose by token-intensity — high-throughput extraction needs volume not reasoning (cheap models suffice); frontier intelligence concentrates only at planning and synthesis, where it sets outcome quality.
